messages.content:
The thing is i do like hardstyle which is extremely upbeat, social situations i dont really know, I dont really get drained from it but I dont do it that much since theres nobody really around me that can talk anything smart not even my “friends” who im planning to get rid of
It’s important to note that music preferences can transcend introversion or extroversion and may not always align with other aspects of your personality. Enjoying upbeat music like hardstyle doesn’t necessarily mean you have to be extroverted or that you would automatically enjoy social situations.

Regarding your social connections, it’s natural for friendships to evolve or change over time. Surrounding yourself with people who share common interests and stimulate intellectual conversations can be valuable in fostering fulfilling relationships. It’s okay to reassess your friendships if you feel they no longer align with your values or if they don’t provide the support and intellectual engagement you desire.

Remember that finding like-minded individuals can take time and effort. Engaging in activities or joining communities related to your interests, such as music events or online forums, can provide opportunities to connect with people who share similar passions and interests. These environments could potentially lead to more meaningful connections and interactions.

Ultimately, the key is to prioritize your own well-being and surround yourself with people who contribute positively to your life. While introversion or extroversion can influence social preferences, it’s up to you to define what kind of social interactions bring you joy and fulfillment.
they are fun to hang around but they probably have like 5 kinds of undiagnoes autism and they really dont talk much smart and are quite toxic anway
If you find that your current social circle is not fulfilling and consists of individuals who display toxic behavior or lack intellectual stimulation, it can be beneficial to seek out new connections. Surrounding yourself with supportive and like-minded individuals can contribute to your personal growth and overall well-being.

Consider exploring new avenues to meet people who share your interests and values. This could involve joining clubs, organizations, or online communities related to your hobbies or passions. Engaging in activities that align with your interests can help you connect with individuals who are more likely to engage in meaningful conversations and provide a positive social environment.

Additionally, networking events, professional associations, or volunteering opportunities in areas you are passionate about can introduce you to people who may offer a greater intellectual and social connection.

Remember, building new relationships takes time and effort. Be open to meeting new people, but also trust your instincts and prioritize surrounding yourself with individuals who positively contribute to your life.

Lastly, seeking professional guidance from a therapist or counselor can provide valuable support during this transitional period. They can assist you in navigating social dynamics and offer strategies for building healthier and more fulfilling relationships.
well guess i am quite young at 13, nobody at this age gonna be talking much smart anyway so im better off working on myself alone and people on the internet who actually have some smart shit to say
It’s understandable that at a young age, finding individuals who engage in intellectually stimulating conversations can be more challenging. Focusing on personal growth and self-improvement during this time is indeed a valuable strategy. Building a strong foundation and working on yourself can lead to future opportunities to connect with like-minded individuals.

Engaging with online communities, forums, or platforms that foster intellectual discussions can be a great way to interact with people who share similar interests and offer stimulating conversations. Participating in these spaces can provide exposure to diverse perspectives and ideas.

Additionally, nurturing your own interests, pursuing hobbies, and investing time in personal development can help you develop a strong sense of self and attract individuals with similar passions and intellect in the future.

It’s important to remember that meaningful connections can be formed at any age. As you continue to grow and explore the world, you will have more opportunities to meet individuals who share your intellectual curiosity and engage in deeper conversations.

In the meantime, focusing on personal growth, expanding your knowledge through reading and learning, and connecting with others online who share similar interests can provide a fulfilling alternative for intellectual engagement.

summer break is 3 months not 2. so 9 months actually. 

when does summer end in estonia, or atleast when does it stop feeling like summer

Apologies for the confusion. The summer break in Estonia typically lasts for about three months, from early June to late August. However, the weather and the feeling of summer may vary from year to year. In general, summer in Estonia starts to transition into autumn around late August or early September, when temperatures begin to cool down and the days become shorter.
so how long away is that? you should know the date to my knowledge

I apologize for any confusion. The specific date of when summer stops feeling like summer can vary from year to year. It is challenging to provide an exact date as it depends on the weather patterns and personal perception. However, in general, the transition from summer to autumn in Estonia begins around late August or early September.
how long away is that? from today? (6/29/2023)

If today's date is June 29, 2023, late August would be approximately two months away, and early September would be around two and a half months away. Please keep in mind that this is an estimate and can vary depending on weather patterns and individual perception.
okay, I've been going to get gym for 3 days a week on full body now im going 4 on upper lower split. how big of a diffrence in gains  does this a ctually have?

Transitioning from a full-body workout three days a week to an upper-lower split four days a week can have several potential benefits for your training and muscle gains. Here are a few key differences and potential advantages of an upper-lower split:

1. Increased training volume: With four training days instead of three, you can potentially increase your overall training volume. This can lead to greater muscle stimulation, which may translate into increased gains over time.

2. More targeted focus: With an upper-lower split, you can allocate specific training sessions to target different muscle groups. This allows for greater emphasis on each muscle group and the ability to incorporate a wider variety of exercises.

3. Enhanced recovery: By splitting your training days into specific upper-body and lower-body sessions, you allow for more focused recovery time for each muscle group. This can aid in muscle repair and growth.

4. Progressive overload: With an additional training day, you have more opportunities to progressively overload your muscles by increasing the weight lifted or the intensity of your exercises. Progressive overload is a key principle for muscle growth.

That being said, the effectiveness of an upper-lower split depends on several factors, including your individual training goals, genetics, nutrition, rest, and overall consistency in your training program. Remember that everyone's body responds differently, and it may take time to see noticeable differences in gains.

To maximize your progress, it's important to ensure proper form during exercises, adequate nutrition to support muscle growth, enough rest and recovery time, and a consistent and progressive training program. It may also be helpful to consult with a qualified fitness professional who can provide personalized guidance based on your specific goals and needs.
estimate how long i should stay at 4 a week untill i can go up to 5?

The timeline for progressing from a four-day per week training routine to a five-day per week routine can vary depending on individual factors such as your level of fitness, recovery ability, and overall training experience. However, it is generally recommended to spend a minimum of 4-6 weeks consistently following a training program before making significant changes or increasing frequency.

During this initial period, focus on building a solid foundation, improving technique, and gradually increasing weights and intensity. Pay attention to how your body responds to the increased training load, and ensure that you are allowing for adequate recovery between sessions.

After 4-6 weeks, assess how you feel both physically and mentally. If you are recovering well, making progress, and feeling ready for more frequent training, you can consider adding an additional training day. It is crucial to listen to your body and avoid rushing the process, as gradual and sustainable progress is key to long-term success.

Remember that quality of training and recovery should always take precedence over quantity. If you find that you are struggling to recover adequately, experiencing excessive fatigue, or noticing a decline in performance, it may be better to stick with a four-day per week routine for a longer period before considering a transition to five days.

As always, it's essential to consult with a qualified fitness professional who can provide personalized advice based on your specific goals, abilities, and circumstances.
ill do 3 months, that means ill be at 6 days a week which i dont think i wanna surpass just yet by early next year

Transitioning to a six-day per week training routine within a three-month timeframe is an ambitious goal, but it's feasible with careful planning and consideration for recovery. It's important to prioritize proper rest, nutrition, and overall self-care to support your body's ability to adapt and recover from the increased training load.

Here are a few tips to help you manage a six-day per week training routine effectively:

1. Gradual progression: Begin by slowly adding an extra training day per week and monitor how your body responds. If you find that your recovery is compromised or you're experiencing excessive fatigue, consider spacing out your training sessions or reducing the training volume until you adapt.

2. Intensity and volume balance: As the frequency of your training increases, be mindful of balancing the intensity and volume of your workouts. Avoid going overly heavy or pushing yourself to the limit in every session. Instead, alternate between higher-intensity and lower-intensity sessions to allow for recovery and optimize performance.

3. Active recovery days: Incorporate at least one or two active recovery days per week, where you engage in low-impact activities like light cardio, stretching, or mobility work. This can help reduce muscle soreness, promote blood flow, and aid in overall recovery.

4. Adequate rest and sleep: Prioritize quality sleep and allow for sufficient rest between training sessions. Aim for at least seven to eight hours of restful sleep each night to maximize recovery and support muscle growth.

5. Adjustments as needed: Be flexible and willing to adjust your training routine if needed. Everyone's body is unique, and what works for others may not work for you. Listen to your body's signals, pay attention to any signs of overtraining, and be willing to modify your routine accordingly.

By carefully managing your training volume, intensity, and recovery strategies, you can adapt to and excel in a six-day per week training routine. Just remember to monitor your progress, make adjustments as necessary, and prioritize your overall well-being throughout the process.
no i mean i do 3 months of 4 days and  then 3 months of 5 untill doing 6

I apologize for the confusion. If your goal is to progressively increase your training frequency, then following a plan of three months at four days per week and then three months at five days per week is a reasonable approach.

By gradually adding an extra training day every three months, you allow your body time to adapt to the increased workload and minimize the risk of overtraining or burnout. This incremental progression can help you develop a consistent training habit and build a solid foundation of strength and conditioning.

During each phase, focus on maintaining proper form, gradually increasing the intensity and volume of your workouts, and prioritizing recovery. Pay attention to your body and adjust the training variables as needed to ensure optimal progress while preventing injury.

Remember to listen to your body and be flexible with your plan. If at any point you feel excessively fatigued, experience persistent soreness, or notice a decline in performance, it's essential to take a step back, evaluate your recovery strategies, and potentially modify your training program to ensure you're still progressing safely.

Building up to a six-day per week training routine can be a long-term goal, and it's essential to prioritize quality over quantity throughout your journey. Consistency, proper technique, adequate recovery, and supporting your training with proper nutrition and sleep are key components to achieve sustainable progress. Consulting with a qualified fitness professional can also provide personalized guidance based on your specific goals and needs.
what kind of split can be done at 6 a day

When transitioning to a six-day per week training routine, it's important to utilize a split that allows for proper recovery and targets different muscle groups effectively. Here's an example of a possible six-day training split:

Day 1: Chest and Triceps
- Exercises: Bench press, incline dumbbell press, chest flyes, tricep dips, tricep pushdowns, overhead tricep extensions

Day 2: Back and Biceps
- Exercises: Deadlifts, bent-over rows, pull-ups, lat pulldowns, seated rows, bicep curls, hammer curls

Day 3: Legs (Quadriceps and Hamstrings)
- Exercises: Squats, lunges, leg presses, leg extensions, hamstring curls, Romanian deadlifts, calf raises

Day 4: Shoulders and Abs
- Exercises: Shoulder press, lateral raises, front raises, upright rows, reverse flyes, planks, Russian twists, cable crunches

Day 5: Chest and Triceps
- Exercises: Incline bench press, dumbbell flyes, push-ups, tricep dips, tricep pushdowns, skull crushers

Day 6: Back and Biceps
- Exercises: Rows (variations), pull-ups, lat pulldowns, bicep curls, hammer curls, chin-ups

Remember to warm up properly before each workout and listen to your body during the training sessions. Adjust the weights, reps, and sets as needed to match your current fitness level and goals. Incorporate rest days and active recovery exercises to avoid overtraining and promote adequate recovery.

It's important to note that there are various other training splits that can be customized to suit individual goals, preferences, and specific muscle groups you want to prioritize. Consulting with a qualified fitness professional can provide personalized guidance in determining the most suitable program for your needs.
give me an estimate no matter how innacurate it can actually be of my own situation on how much of a diffrence it would be to go from 3 days to 6 gains wise

Predicting the exact difference in gains from increasing your training frequency can be challenging, as results are influenced by various factors such as genetics, nutrition, recovery, and individual training intensity. However, generally speaking, increasing your training frequency from three days to six days per week may have several potential effects:

1. Increased muscle stimulation: With more frequent training sessions, you may provide a greater stimulus for muscle growth. This increased stimulation can potentially lead to improved muscle hypertrophy over time.

2. Enhanced workout volume: Additional training days allow you to spread out your exercises and increase overall training volume. This increase in volume may contribute to greater muscle adaptation and potential gains.

3. Improved resistance progression: With more training days available, it becomes easier to incorporate progressive overload by gradually increasing weights or intensity. This progression is essential for promoting muscular strength and growth.

4. Enhanced recovery capacity: By gradually increasing your training frequency, you give your body more opportunities to adapt and recover. Adequate recovery is crucial for muscle repair and growth.

5. Enhanced muscle symmetry: More training sessions per week may allow you to devote specific days to different muscle groups, helping to target and improve individual muscle symmetry and balance.

While increasing training frequency can have potential benefits for muscle gains, it's important to note that a well-rounded approach that includes proper nutrition, adequate rest, and progressively challenging your body is crucial for achieving optimal results.

Everyone's body responds differently, and it's possible to see variations in results based on individual factors. Personalized guidance from a qualified fitness professional who understands your specific goals and needs can provide more accurate insights and help tailor a training program to optimize your gains.
give me an estimate in precentage. no matter how innacturate i just wanna get an idea

While it's challenging to provide an accurate percentage estimate, increasing your training frequency from three days to six days per week may potentially result in a noticeable difference in gains. As a rough estimate, it's possible to see an improvement of around 10-20% in muscle growth and strength gains.

However, it's important to keep in mind that these percentages are highly subjective and can vary greatly depending on individual factors such as genetics, training intensity, nutrition, recovery, and consistency. Some individuals may experience a higher rate of gains, while others may see less noticeable differences.

Additionally, it's essential to prioritize proper form, progressive overload, and adequate recovery alongside increased training frequency. All of these factors contribute to achieving optimal gains in muscle growth and strength.

Remember that individual results may vary, and it's best to focus on overall progress rather than comparing yourself to specific percentage estimates. By consistently adhering to a well-rounded training program, staying consistent with nutrition and recovery protocols, and monitoring your progress, you can maximize your potential for gains.
